questions.prompt: Read: 'Penicillin ___ (discover) by Alexander Fleming in 1928. Since then, countless lives ___ (save) by antibiotics.' Choose the correct pair.

options.text: was discovered / have been saved
was discovered / were saved
has been discovered / have been saved
is discovered / are saved

questions.explanation: 'Was discovered' (past simple passive) is correct for 1928 — a specific past date. 'Have been saved' (present perfect passive) is correct for 'since then' — a period from the past continuing to now. Option B uses past simple for both, but 'since then' requires present perfect. Option C uses present perfect for the discovery in 1928 — wrong, since 'in 1928' forces past simple. Option D uses present tenses — completely wrong for historical facts.

questions.prompt: Four journalists wrote headlines. Which uses the passive MOST appropriately for a formal news headline?

options.text: Scientists discover new planet.
Someone hacked the national database last night.
New planet discovered by science team.
The national database got hacked last night.

questions.explanation: 'New planet discovered' is the most appropriate formal passive headline — it puts the discovery (the topic) first and names the agent only if useful. Option A (active) is also acceptable in news writing but puts the scientists first, which is less common for major discoveries. Option B names 'someone' — vague agent, so passive would be more appropriate here. Option D uses the get-passive ('got hacked') which is too informal for a formal news outlet.

questions.prompt: Which sentence contains a HIDDEN ERROR in passive construction?

options.text: The results will be published next Monday.
Several complaints have been made by customers.
The data was being analysed when the system crashed.
The suspect was saw entering the building on camera.

questions.explanation: 'Was saw' is incorrect — 'see' has the irregular past participle 'seen', not 'saw' ('saw' is the simple past). The correct form is 'was seen entering the building'. This is a common learner error: confusing the past simple form ('saw') with the past participle ('seen') needed for the passive. Options A, B, and C all use correct passive forms.

questions.prompt: Read: 'The charity ___ by three volunteers in a small flat. Today it ___ by a team of 200 people worldwide.' What pair best completes this contrast?

options.text: is founded / is run
was founded / is run
had been founded / has been run
was founded / was run

questions.explanation: 'Was founded' (past simple passive) describes the historical founding event. 'Is run' (present simple passive) describes the current ongoing situation ('today'). This combination perfectly expresses the contrast between past origin and present state. Option A uses present for both — wrong, as the founding is historical. Option C uses past perfect and present perfect — awkward and overly complex. Option D uses past tense for 'today' — contradicts 'today'.

questions.prompt: Which explanation CORRECTLY describes why the passive is used in academic writing?

options.text: The passive is used because academic writers prefer longer sentences.
The passive creates an impersonal, objective tone by removing the agent ('I', 'we') from the sentence.
The passive is required by grammar rules whenever writing about science.
The passive is used to make the reader do more work to understand.

questions.explanation: In academic writing, the passive 'The results were analysed' is preferred over 'We analysed the results' because it creates impersonal, objective-sounding prose — the focus is on the process, not on the researchers. This is the standard convention in scientific reporting. Option A is a misunderstanding — length is not the goal. Option C is false — the passive is a stylistic choice, not a grammar rule. Option D is incorrect — good academic writing aims for clarity.

questions.prompt: Read the error-correction task: 'The new shopping centre was opened last week. It was visited by thousands of people on the first day. The car park was become full within an hour.' What needs fixing?

options.text: 'was visited' should be 'has been visited' — it connects to the present.
'was opened' should be 'has been opened' — last week is recent.
'was become' is incorrect — 'become' is intransitive and cannot be passivised. Use 'became' instead.
No errors — all three sentences use the passive correctly.

questions.explanation: 'Was become' is the error: 'become' is an intransitive verb (it has no object) and cannot be passivised. The correct sentence is 'The car park became full within an hour' (active, past simple). Options A and B incorrectly suggest switching tenses — 'last week' requires past simple, so 'was visited' and 'was opened' are both correct. Option D is wrong because the third sentence does contain an error.
